452	\subsection{The candidate inverse}
453	
454	\begin{definition}\leanok
455	[Overlap base change in pullback form]
456	  \label{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}
457	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapFactorIso}
458	  \uses{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
459	  Conjugating the overlap base change \(\beta_{ij}\)
460	  (\cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}) on both sides by the comparison
461	  \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib} between the site-level
462	  restriction and the geometric pullback yields the isomorphism, in geometric form,
463	  \[
464	    \gamma_{ij} \;:\;
465	      \iota_i^{*}\bigl((\iota_j)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j\bigr)
466	        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
467	      (f_{ij})_{*}\bigl((t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j\bigr),
468	  \]
469	  evaluated at the input sheaf \(\mathcal{M}_j\).
470	\end{definition}
471	
472	\begin{definition}\leanok
473	[The \(j\)-th component of the candidate inverse]
474	  \label{def:gr_glueChartComponent}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartComponent}
476	  \uses{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}
477	  Given the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij} : f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i \xrightarrow{\sim}
478	  (t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), the \(j\)-th component of the candidate
479	  inverse \(\mathrm{s}_i\) is the composite
480	  \[
481	    \mathcal{M}_i
482	      \xrightarrow{\ \eta_{f_{ij}}\ }
483	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i
484	      \xrightarrow{\ (f_{ij})_{*}g_{ij}\ }
485	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j
486	      \xrightarrow{\ \gamma_{ij}^{-1}\ }
487	    \iota_i^{*}\bigl((\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\bigr),
488	  \]
489	  i.e. the geometric unit along \(f_{ij}\), the pushforward of the transition
490	  \(g_{ij}\), and the inverse of \(\gamma_{ij}\) (\cref{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}).
491	\end{definition}
492	
493	\begin{definition}\leanok
494	[The candidate-inverse family]
495	  \label{def:gr_glueChartFamily}
496	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartFamily}
497	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:gr_glue_equalizer}
498	  Assembling the components \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent} through the
499	  product-preservation comparison of \(\iota_i^{*}\) on the pushforward product
500	  \(P = \prod_j (\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) (\cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}) gives a
501	  single morphism
502	  \[
503	    \mathcal{M}_i \;\longrightarrow\; \iota_i^{*}P,
504	  \]
505	  the candidate inverse before it is corrected to land in the equalizer
506	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\).
507	\end{definition}
508	
509	\subsection{The triple-overlap toolkit}
510	
511	The first descent obligation (\cref{lem:gr_glueChartFamily_equalizes}) reduces, factor
512	by factor of the restricted overlap product, to a single equation over a \emph{triple
513	overlap}. This subsection records that machinery. Fix three chart indices \(i, p, q\)
514	and form the triple overlap
515	\[
516	  V_{ipq} \;=\; V_{ip} \times_{U_i} V_{iq},
517	\]
518	with first projection \(\mathrm{fst} : V_{ipq} \to V_{ip}\). Two morphisms out of
519	\(V_{ipq}\) organise everything: the \emph{chart leg}
520	\(q_{pq} = \mathrm{fst}\circ f_{ip} : V_{ipq} \to U_i\) (down to \(U_i\)), and the
521	\emph{transport leg} \(\tau = t'_{ipq}\circ \mathrm{fst}_{pq} : V_{ipq} \to V_{pq}\)
522	(across to the pair overlap, through the cocycle transport \(t'_{ipq}\)). The square
523	\[
524	  \begin{array}{ccc}
525	    V_{ipq} & \xrightarrow{\ \ \tau\ \ } & V_{pq} \\
526	    \ \downarrow{\scriptstyle q_{pq}} & & \downarrow{\scriptstyle f_{pq}\circ \iota_p} \\
527	    U_i & \xrightarrow{\ \ \iota_i\ \ } & X
528	  \end{array}
529	\]
530	is the triple analogue of the chart--overlap square of \cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}.
531	
532	\begin{lemma}
533	[Triple-overlap bridges of the glue datum]
534	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
536	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
537	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
538	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue}
539	  On the triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij}\times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the following three
540	  identities of morphisms hold, lining up the endpoints of the three base-change
541	  transports of the cocycle equation (C2):
542	  \begin{enumerate}
543	    \item \emph{(source)} the two projections of \(V_{ijk}\) to \(V_{ij}\) and \(V_{ik}\),
544	      followed by the overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\) and \(f_{ik}\) into \(U_i\),
545	      agree;
546	    \item \emph{(middle)} the \(ij\)-transport's target leg
547	      \((t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})\) and the \(jk\)-transport's source leg
548	      (through the cocycle transport \(t'_{ijk}\) and \(f_{jk}\)) agree as morphisms
549	      to \(U_j\);
550	    \item \emph{(target)} the composite \(jk\)-after-\(ij\) transport's target leg
551	      \((t_{jk}\circ f_{kj})\) and the \(ik\)-transport's target leg
552	      \((t_{ik}\circ f_{ki})\) agree as morphisms to \(U_k\).
553	  \end{enumerate}
554	\end{lemma}
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue}
557	  The source identity is the universal property (pullback condition) of \(V_{ijk}\).
558	  The middle identity follows from the transition factorisation
559	  \(t'_{ijk}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} together with the pullback condition.
560	  The target identity -- the heart of the cocycle -- combines the same transition
561	  factorisation, the pullback condition, the involutivity \(t_{ik}\circ t_{ki}=\mathrm{id}\)
562	  and the cocycle condition of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}.
563	\end{proof}
564	
565	\begin{lemma}
566	[Commutativity of the triple-overlap square]
567	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}
568	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_triple_square}
569	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:scheme_modules_glue}
570	  The triple-overlap square above commutes: the transport leg \(\tau\) followed by
571	  \(f_{pq}\circ \iota_p\) equals the chart leg \(q_{pq}\) followed by \(\iota_i\), as
572	  morphisms \(V_{ipq}\to X\).
573	\end{lemma}
574	\begin{proof}
575	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:scheme_modules_glue}
576	  Substitute the middle bridge \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges} to rewrite the transport
577	  leg, then apply the glue condition \((t_{ip}\circ f_{pi})\circ\iota_p =
578	  f_{ip}\circ\iota_i\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}.
579	\end{proof}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	[Triple-overlap opens identity]
583	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}
584	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_preimage_image_eq₃}
585	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_bridges, lem:gr_glueData_triple_square, def:scheme_modules_glue}
586	  For an open \(V \subseteq U_i\), the preimage under \(f_{pq}\circ\iota_p\) of the image
587	  \(\iota_i(V)\) in the glued scheme equals the image, under the transport leg \(\tau\),
588	  of the preimage \(q_{pq}^{-1}(V)\):
589	  \[
590	    (f_{pq}\circ\iota_p)^{-1}\bigl(\iota_i(V)\bigr)
591	      \;=\; \tau\bigl(q_{pq}^{-1}(V)\bigr).
592	  \]
593	  This is the underlying-opens cartesianness of the triple-overlap square.
594	\end{lemma}
595	\begin{proof}
596	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_bridges, lem:gr_glueData_triple_square, def:scheme_modules_glue}
597	  A point of \(V_{pq}\) lands in \(\iota_i(V)\) precisely when it is glued, through the
598	  overlap \(V_{ip}\), to a point of \(U_i\) lying in \(V\); the gluing-incidence
599	  criterion at the pairs \((i,p)\) and \((i,q)\) certifies that such a point lifts to
600	  \(V_{ipq}\), and injectivity of the open immersion \(f_{pq}\) pins down the transport,
601	  using the middle bridge \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}. The reverse inclusion is the
602	  commutativity \cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}.
603	\end{proof}
604	
605	\begin{lemma}
606	[The two composite opens functors of the triple square agree]
607	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_triple_opensFunctor_eq}
608	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_triple_opensFunctor_eq}
609	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}
610	  The composite opens functor ``into the glued scheme along \(\iota_i\), then back to
611	  \(V_{pq}\) along \(f_{pq}\circ\iota_p\)'' equals the composite ``down to \(V_{ipq}\)
612	  along \(q_{pq}\), then across to \(V_{pq}\) along \(\tau\)''.
613	\end{lemma}
614	\begin{proof}
615	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}
616	  The two functors agree on objects by the opens identity
617	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}, and on morphisms because the connecting arrows
618	  of the opens poset are proof-irrelevant.
619	\end{proof}
620	
621	\begin{lemma}
622	[Structure-sheaf compatibility of the triple square]
623	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_triple_appIso_compat}
624	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_triple_appIso_compat}
625	  \uses{lem:gr_appLE_congr_mor, lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}
626	  For an open \(V \subseteq U_i\) the two composite section maps
627	  \(\Gamma(U_i, V) \to \Gamma\bigl(V_{pq}, \tau(q_{pq}^{-1}(V))\bigr)\) of the triple
628	  square -- ``through the glued scheme'' and ``through the triple overlap'' -- coincide.
629	\end{lemma}
630	\begin{proof}
631	  \uses{lem:gr_appLE_congr_mor, lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}
632	  Both composites are the \(\mathrm{appLE}\) of the two (equal, by
633	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}) composite morphisms of the square; the equality
634	  is then \cref{lem:gr_appLE_congr_mor}, exactly as in the pair-level
635	  \cref{lem:gr_overlap_appIso_compat}.
636	\end{proof}
637	
638	\begin{lemma}
639	[Triple-overlap base change (\(\beta_{ipq}\))]
640	  \label{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueTripleBaseChangeIso}
642	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage, lem:gr_glueData_triple_opensFunctor_eq,
643	    lem:gr_glueData_triple_appIso_compat}
644	  There is a natural isomorphism of functors
645	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_{V_{pq}}) \to \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ipq}})\),
646	  \[
647	    \beta_{ipq} \;:\;
648	      (f_{pq}\circ\iota_p)_{*}\circ \iota_i^{*}
649	        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
650	      \tau^{*}\circ (q_{pq})_{*},
651	  \]
652	  identifying the chart restriction of a pushforward from the pair overlap \(V_{pq}\)
653	  with the pushforward along \(q_{pq}\) of the restriction along \(\tau\). It is
654	  assembled by the same four-factor recipe as the pair-level overlap base change
655	  (\cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}) over the opens identity
656	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}.
657	\end{lemma}
658	\begin{proof}
659	  \uses{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage, lem:gr_glueData_triple_opensFunctor_eq,
660	    lem:gr_glueData_triple_appIso_compat}
661	  Both sides are site-level pushforwards along the two equal composite opens functors
662	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_opensFunctor_eq}; the comparison is the pushforward-com\-position
663	  isomorphisms together with the transport of one opens functor to the other, with the
664	  section-level coherence supplied by \cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_appIso_compat}.
665	\end{proof}
666	
667	\begin{lemma}
668	[Sections of the triple base change, inverse side]
669	  \label{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso_inv_app}
670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueTripleBaseChangeIso_inv_app_app}
671	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso, lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}

679	  single presheaf restriction along the opens identity; composing leaves exactly the

683	\begin{lemma}
684	[Sections of the triple base change, forward side]
685	  \label{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso_hom_app}
686	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueTripleBaseChangeIso_hom_app_app}
687	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso, lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}
688	  Symmetrically, on sections over \(V\subseteq U_i\) the forward \(\beta_{ipq}\) is the

691	\end{lemma}
692	\begin{proof}
693	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso, lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}

697	
698	\begin{lemma}
699	[Triple-overlap base change in pullback form]
700	  \label{lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso}
701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueTripleFactorIso}
702	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
703	  For a sheaf \(\mathcal{N}\) on the pair overlap \(V_{pq}\), conjugating \(\beta_{ipq}\)
704	  on both sides by the comparison between site-level restriction and geometric pullback
705	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}) yields the geometric-form
706	  isomorphism
707	  \[
708	    \iota_i^{*}\bigl((f_{pq}\circ\iota_p)_{*}\mathcal{N}\bigr)
709	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
710	    (q_{pq})_{*}\bigl(\tau^{*}\mathcal{N}\bigr).
711	  \]
712	\end{lemma}
713	\begin{proof}
714	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}

716	  comparison \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib} on the chart factor
717	  and by the same comparison pushed forward along \(q_{pq}\) on the transport factor.

719	
720	\begin{lemma}
721	[Transpose of the triple base change (mate core)]
722	  \label{lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_transpose}
723	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueTripleFactor_transpose}
724	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso, lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso,
725	    lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso, lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage,
726	    lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}
727	  The adjoint transpose along \(\iota_i\) of the pullback-form triple base change
728	  \cref{lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso} is the canonical four-functor comparison of the
729	  triple square: the unit along \(\tau\) pushed forward along \(f_{pq}\circ\iota_p\),
730	  regrouped by the pushforward-composition comparison, reindexed by the
731	  pushforward-congruence along \cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}, and ungrouped by the
732	  inverse pushforward-composition. The identity is free of the cocycle hypotheses.
733	\end{lemma}
734	\begin{proof}
735	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso, lem:gr_glueTripleBaseChangeIso,
736	    lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso, lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}
737	  Verbatim the proof of the pair-level \cref{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_transpose} with the
738	  substitutions \(\iota_j \rightsquigarrow f_{pq}\circ\iota_p\),
739	  \(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}\rightsquigarrow \tau\), \(f_{ij}\rightsquigarrow q_{pq}\): the
740	  comparison isomorphisms conjugating the chart factor cancel against unit/counit by
741	  \cref{lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso}, leaving \(\beta_{ipq}\), whose section form
742	  (\cref{lem:gr_glueData_triple_preimage}) folds the residual cycle of restrictions to
743	  the stated four-factor composite.
744	\end{proof}

746	\begin{lemma}
747	[Mate recognition of the triple factor transpose]
748	  \label{lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_mate}
749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueTripleFactor_mate}
750	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_transpose, lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app,
751	    lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib, lem:gr_glueData_triple_square}
752	  For any morphism \(m : \mathcal{W} \to (f_{pq}\circ\iota_p)_{*}\mathcal{N}\) on the
753	  glued scheme, the conjugated transport of the inverse transpose of \(m\) along the
754	  triple square equals the inverse transpose, along \(q_{pq}\), of \(\iota_i^{*}(m)\)
755	  followed by the pullback-form triple base change \cref{lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso}. In
756	  other words, \cref{lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_transpose} is the mate of
757	  \cref{lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso} through the pullback pseudofunctor.
758	\end{lemma}

760	  \uses{lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_transpose, lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app,
761	    lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
762	  By injectivity of the two transpose bijections compare the double transposes;
763	  naturality of the transpose in its left argument peels \(m\) off, the conjugate of the
764	  pullback-composition isomorphism along the triple square
765	  (\cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app},
766	  \cref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}) is the inverse
767	  pushforward-composition isomorphism, and folding the unit pair reduces the claim to the
768	  mate core \cref{lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_transpose} -- the same proof as the pair-level
769	  \cref{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate}.
770	\end{proof}

772	\begin{lemma}
773	[Transpose of the first descent-leg factor]
774	  \label{lem:gr_glueLegA_component_transpose}

776	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer}

785	  composite-unit identity; the transpose of the counit is the identity by the right
786	  triangle identity of the adjunction, which consumes the inner factor.
787	\end{proof}

791	  \label{lem:gr_glueLegB_component_transpose}

793	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer, def:scheme_modules_glue}

802	  As in \cref{lem:gr_glueLegA_component_transpose}, with two extra moves: naturality of

807	\begin{lemma}
808	[The candidate-inverse family recovers its components]
809	  \label{lem:gr_glueChartFamily_pullback_map_pi}
810	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartFamily_pullback_map_π}
811	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartFamily, def:gr_glueChartComponent}
812	  The candidate-inverse family \cref{def:gr_glueChartFamily} followed by the chart
813	  pullback of the \(j\)-th product projection recovers the \(j\)-th component
814	  \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent}:
815	  \[
816	    \mathrm{s}_i\ \circ\ \iota_i^{*}(\mathrm{pr}_j) \;=\; \mathrm{s}_i^{(j)}.
817	  \]
818	\end{lemma}
819	\begin{proof}
820	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartFamily, def:gr_glueChartComponent}
821	  Unfold the family \cref{def:gr_glueChartFamily} as a product lift composed with the
822	  inverse product-preservation comparison; the comparison cancels against the lift,
823	  leaving the defining \(j\)-th component \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent}.
824	\end{proof}

826	\begin{lemma}
827	[Pair component of the equalizing condition (C2 over the triple overlap)]
828	  \label{lem:gr_glueChartComponent_leg_compat}
829	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartComponent_leg_compat}
830	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso,
831	    lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso, lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_mate,
832	    lem:gr_glueLegA_component_transpose, lem:gr_glueLegB_component_transpose,
833	    lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
834	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom,
835	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
836	  For chart indices \(i, p, q\), the \(p\)-th candidate-inverse component
837	  \cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent} followed by the chart pullback of the first
838	  descent-leg factor equals the \(q\)-th component followed by the chart pullback of the
839	  second descent-leg factor. This is the \((p,q)\)-component of the first descent
840	  obligation, where the triple-overlap multiplicativity (C2) of
841	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} at \((i,p,q)\) is consumed.
842	\end{lemma}
843	\begin{proof}
844	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso,
845	    lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso, lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_mate,
846	    lem:gr_glueLegA_component_transpose, lem:gr_glueLegB_component_transpose,
847	    lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
848	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom,
849	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
850	  \emph{Reduction.} Cancel the triple base change in pullback form
851	  \cref{lem:gr_glueTripleFactorIso} (an isomorphism) from the right and transpose the
852	  equation along the chart leg \(q_{pq}\) (the transpose is a bijection). On both sides
853	  fire the triple mate \cref{lem:gr_glueTripleFactor_mate} and substitute the leg
854	  transposes \cref{lem:gr_glueLegA_component_transpose},
855	  \cref{lem:gr_glueLegB_component_transpose}. This reduces the claim to the
856	  \emph{conjugated cocycle equation} over \(V_{ipq}\): the chart pullbacks of the two
857	  components, base-change--transported and re-indexed, agree.
858	
859	  \emph{The conjugated cocycle.} Expand each component
860	  \[
861	    \mathrm{s}_i^{(p)}
862	      \;=\; \eta_{f_{ip}}\ \circ\ (f_{ip})_{*}g_{ip}\ \circ\ \gamma_{ip}^{-1}
863	  \]
864	  (\cref{def:gr_glueChartComponent}) and pull it back along \(q_{pq} = \mathrm{fst}\circ
865	  f_{ip}\). Three moves clear the remaining factors:
866	  \begin{enumerate}
867	    \item the chart pullback \(q_{pq}^{*}\) of the inverse overlap factor
868	      \(\gamma_{ip}^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}) is converted into
869	      pullback-composition casts and transposes by the \emph{pair} mate
870	      \cref{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_mate} at \((i,p)\);
871	    \item the chart pullback of the unit \(\eta_{f_{ip}}\) along the morphism
872	      \(q_{pq}\), which factors through \(f_{ip}\), is consumed by the triangle identity
873	      of the adjunction threaded through the pullback-composition cast at
874	      \((\mathrm{fst}, f_{ip})\) -- this is exactly the step that cancels the residual
875	      \(\tau^{*}(f_{pq}^{*}\varepsilon_p)\) factor produced on the leg-A side;
876	    \item what remains is the cocycle multiplicativity (C2) of
877	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} at the triple \((i,p,q)\), conjugated by the
878	      base-change transports \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}; the
879	      triple bridges \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges} were chosen precisely so that the
880	      \(\mathrm{pullbackCongr}\) casts on the two sides line up the endpoints on the
881	      nose.
882	  \end{enumerate}
883	  The degenerate pairs \(p = i\) or \(q = i\) need no separate case split: (C2) holds for
884	  all triples, and where endpoint alignment forces it the self-identity (C1) enters
885	  through the congruence cast \cref{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom}.
886	\end{proof}

890	\begin{lemma}\leanok
891	[The candidate-inverse family equalizes the restricted legs (C2 transported)]
892	  \label{lem:gr_glueChartFamily_equalizes}
893	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueChartFamily_equalizes}
894	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartFamily, def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
895	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_overlap_appIso_compat,
896	    lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
897	  The family \cref{def:gr_glueChartFamily} equalizes the two restricted descent legs
898	  \(\iota_i^{*}a\) and \(\iota_i^{*}b\); equivalently, it lands in the restricted
899	  equalizer \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\). This is the first descent obligation, where the
900	  triple-overlap multiplicativity (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} is consumed.
901	\end{lemma}
902	\begin{proof}
903	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartFamily, lem:gr_glueChartFamily_pullback_map_pi,
904	    lem:gr_glueChartComponent_leg_compat}
905	  It suffices to check equality after projecting to each factor \((p,q)\) of the
906	  restricted overlap product, through the product-preservation comparison of
907	  \(\iota_i^{*}\) (the same comparison discipline as \cref{lem:gr_glueRestrict_proj_compat}).
908	  Folding each restricted leg against the \((p,q)\)-projection -- splitting off the
909	  \(p\)- and \(q\)-th chart projections and cancelling the comparison via
910	  \cref{lem:gr_glueChartFamily_pullback_map_pi} -- turns the \((p,q)\)-component into the
911	  equation
912	  \[
913	    \mathrm{s}_i^{(p)}\ \circ\ \iota_i^{*}\bigl(a_{(p,q)}\bigr)
914	      \;=\;
915	    \mathrm{s}_i^{(q)}\ \circ\ \iota_i^{*}\bigl(b_{(p,q)}\bigr),
916	  \]
917	  which is precisely the pair-component obligation
918	  \cref{lem:gr_glueChartComponent_leg_compat}, where the triple-overlap multiplicativity
919	  (C2) is consumed. Assembling these componentwise equalities back through the comparison
920	  gives the equalizing condition.
921	\end{proof}

943	  restricted equalizer inclusion of \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) into \(\iota_i^{*}P\)

954	  is \(\iota_i^{*}(\pi_j)\).

1001	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:scheme_modules_glue,
1002	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_congr, lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso,
1003	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_counit, lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app,

1006	  chart immersion \(\iota_i\) (post-composed with the geometric counit), is the
1007	  identity of \(\mathcal{M}_i\):
1008	  \[
1009	    \mathrm{s}_i^{(i)}\ \circ\ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{geo}}_{\mathcal{M}_i}
1010	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{M}_i}.

1014	\begin{proof}
1015	  \uses{def:gr_glueChartComponent, def:scheme_modules_glue,
1016	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_congr, lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso,
1017	    lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_counit, lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app,
1018	    lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom}
1019	  Since \(f_{ii}\) factors as \(t_{ii}\circ f_{ii}\) (because \(t_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\)),
1020	  (C1) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} says the transition \(g_{ii}\) is the
1021	  canonical congruence cast; through \cref{lem:gr_pullbackCongr_hom_eqToHom} and
1022	  \cref{lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_congr} the three middle factors of the component
1023	  collapse to a single site-level congruence cast. Rewriting the geometric unit by
1024	  \cref{lem:gr_restrictAdjunction_unit_iso} and the geometric counit by
1025	  \cref{lem:gr_restrictIsoPullback_counit} turns the whole composite into the
1026	  site-level unit, the congruence cast, the section form of \(\beta_{ii}^{-1}\)
1027	  (\cref{lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_inv_app}), and the site-level counit. On sections
1028	  over an open this is a cycle of presheaf restrictions along opens identities whose
1029	  composite open-inclusion is the identity; folding the restrictions and using
1030	  proof-irrelevance of the open inclusions gives the identity.
1031	\end{proof}

1034	[Transpose of the overlap base change (mate core)]
1035	  \label{lem:gr_glueOverlapFactor_transpose}

1040	  \(\gamma_{ij}\) (\cref{def:gr_glueOverlapFactorIso}) is the canonical four-functor
1041	  comparison of the glue square:
1042	  \[
1043	    \widehat{\gamma_{ij}}
1044	      \;=\;
1045	    (\iota_j)_{*}\bigl(\eta_{t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}}\bigr)
1046	      \ \circ\ \mathrm{pushComp}
1047	      \ \circ\ \mathrm{pushCongr}
1048	      \ \circ\ \mathrm{pushComp}^{-1},
1049	  \]
1050	  the unit along \(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}\) pushed forward along \(\iota_j\), regrouped by
1051	  the pushforward-composition comparison, reindexed by the pushforward-congruence along
1052	  the glue condition \((t_{ij}\circ f_{ji})\circ \iota_j = f_{ij}\circ \iota_i\), and
1053	  ungrouped by the inverse pushforward-composition. The identity is free of the
1054	  cocycle hypotheses: it is pure site-level content.

1059	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}

1061	  isomorphisms (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}) conjugating the
1062	  chart factor cancel against the unit/counit, leaving the bare site-level overlap base
1063	  change \(\beta_{ij}\) (\cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}). The unit bridge at
1064	  \(t_{ij}\circ f_{ji}\) and the three naturality squares of the pushforward-composition
1065	  and pushforward-congruence comparisons rewrite the transpose into the stated
1066	  four-factor composite up to whiskering and reassociation. The residual site-level
1067	  core is checked on sections over an open: by \cref{lem:gr_overlapBaseChange_hom_app}
1068	  the section form of \(\beta_{ij}\) is a presheaf restriction along an opens identity,
1069	  and the remaining composite is a cycle of such restrictions whose total open
1070	  inclusion is the identity, folded by proof-irrelevance exactly as in the closing step
1071	  of \cref{lem:gr_glueChartComponent_self_counit}.

1151	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1152	[Chart restrictions are jointly faithful]
1153	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_jointly_faithful}
1154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_map_jointly_faithful}
1155	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib,
1156	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
1157	  Two morphisms \(u, v : \mathcal{W} \to \mathcal{W}'\) of sheaves of modules on the
1158	  glued scheme that agree after pullback to every chart, \(\iota_i^{*}u = \iota_i^{*}v\)
1159	  for all \(i\), are equal. This realises the restriction-uniqueness core of the
1160	  characterisation of the glued sheaf up to unique isomorphism: a morphism out of the
1161	  glued sheaf is determined chart-locally.
1162	\end{lemma}
1163	\begin{proof}
1164	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
1165	  Transfer the chart agreement to the site-level restriction functor by conjugating
1166	  with the comparison \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}, whose
1167	  sections are concrete: \(\Gamma(\iota_i^{\mathrm{res}}\mathcal{W}, V) =
1168	  \Gamma(\mathcal{W}, \iota_i(V))\). Then test \(u\) and \(v\) on sections over any
1169	  open \(O\) of the glued scheme using separatedness of the target sheaf
1170	  (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}) over the cover \(\{\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}O)\}\)
1171	  of \(O\) — which is a cover by joint surjectivity of the chart immersions
1172	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}). On each cover piece the agreement is the chart
1173	  identity moved inside the sections by naturality of \(u, v\) on the presheaf level.
1174	\end{proof}

1189	  transition family \((g_{ij})\) satisfying (C1)/(C2). For each chart index \(i\) the

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
178	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
179	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
180	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
181	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
182	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
183	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
184	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
185	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
186	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
187	\end{proof}
188	
189	% HISTORICAL (abandoned route): an earlier draft built the glued sheaf as a hand-rolled
190	% presheaf of compatible families (decls gluePresheaf / gluePresheafModule / gluePresheafIsSheaf).
191	% That route is abandoned; the gluing is now the equalizer of pushforwards described in the
192	% Construction paragraph of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, which subsumes it.
193	
194	\begin{definition}
195	
196	\leanok
197	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
198	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
200	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
201	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
202	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
203	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
204	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
205	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
206	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
207	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
208	  charts. Suppose given:
209	  \begin{itemize}
210	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
211	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
212	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
213	      \[
214	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
215	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
216	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
217	      \]
218	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
219	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
220	  \end{itemize}
221	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
222	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
223	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
224	  \((g_{ij})\):
225	  \begin{enumerate}
226	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
227	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
228	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
229	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
230	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
231	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
232	      isomorphisms satisfy
233	      \[
234	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
235	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
236	      \]
237	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
238	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
239	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
240	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
241	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
242	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
243	      condition.
244	  \end{enumerate}
245	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
246	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing is well-defined. Out of this
247	  data the construction produces a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
248	  \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\).
249	
250	  \emph{Construction.} The category \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
251	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has all limits, and both pushforward along a morphism and
252	  these limits preserve the sheaf condition; the glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is therefore
253	  built directly as a limit -- an \emph{equalizer of pushforwards} -- rather than through a
254	  hand-built presheaf of compatible families. Concretely, form the two parallel maps of
255	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
256	  \[
257	    \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
258	      \;\;\underset{b}{\overset{a}{\rightrightarrows}}\;\;
259	    \prod_{i,j} (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\,\bigl(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr),
260	  \]
261	  where, on the \((i,j)\)-component, leg \(a\) pushes the \(i\)-th factor forward along the
262	  overlap immersion \(f_{ij}\) using the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction
263	  together with the pushforward-composition comparison
264	  \((\iota_i)_{*} \cong (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\circ f_{ij}^{*}\) (the dual of
265	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), and leg \(b\) pushes the \(j\)-th factor forward and
266	  additionally transports it across the inverse transition isomorphism \((g_{ij})^{-1}\)
267	  and the glue datum's compatibility identity, so that both legs land on the common overlap
268	  sheaf \((\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i)\). The glued sheaf is the
269	  equalizer
270	  \[
271	    \mathcal{M} \;=\; \operatorname{eq}(a,b) \;\hookrightarrow\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i,
272	  \]
273	  the sub-sheaf of tuples of pushed-forward chart sections whose two overlap restrictions
274	  agree -- precisely the compatible families, now realised as a categorical limit rather than
275	  a bespoke presheaf. The cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2) on the family \((g_{ij})\) are
276	  \emph{not} consumed in forming this equalizer object: the limit exists for any family of
277	  transition maps. They are instead what make the equalizer descend correctly, and are
278	  exactly the hypotheses pinned down downstream when the restriction isomorphisms
279	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso} are produced. This specialises effective descent
280	  of sheaves of modules to the concrete open cover \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\)
281	  supplied by a scheme glue datum, and is the form in which the Grassmannian's
282	  tautological bundle and quotient are built.
283	
284	  The glued sheaf comes with three further structures, each recorded as its own block
285	  downstream: the \emph{restriction isomorphisms} \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}
286	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps
287	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}; the \emph{characterisation up to unique
288	  isomorphism} by the restriction property \cref{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}; and the
289	  \emph{descent of morphisms} -- a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
290	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms glues to a unique \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to
291	  \mathcal{N}\) with \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ
292	  \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\) \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}.
293	\end{definition}
294	
295	\begin{definition}[Restriction isomorphisms of the glued sheaf]
296	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}
297	  % NOTE: the Lean decl `glueRestrictionIso` is pinned canonically by
298	  % `def:glueRestrictionIso` in Picard_GlueDescent.tex (its 1:1 home after the
299	  % iter-067 file split). This block is the downstream consumer's cross-reference
300	  % node; it carries no \lean{} pin to avoid a duplicate.
301	  \uses{def:glueRestrictionIso, def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
302	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
303	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. For each
304	  chart index \(i\) there is a \emph{restriction isomorphism} of sheaves of
305	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
306	  \[
307	    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,
308	  \]
309	  compatible with the transition isomorphisms: over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two
310	  restrictions \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\), transported to the common overlap, differ
311	  exactly by \(g_{ij}\).
312	\end{definition}
313	\begin{proof}
314	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
315	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
316	  By the equalizer-of-pushforwards description \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, a section of
317	  \(\mathcal{M}\) over an open of the form \(\iota_i^{-1}V\), restricted to the chart
318	  \(U_i\), is recovered from and recovers its \(i\)-th component
319	  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \cdot)\) -- the \(i\)-th factor of the product
320	  \(\prod_i (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) into which the equalizer embeds; the
321	  chart-restriction bridge \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso} identifies the pullback
322	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) with this restriction, and the \(i\)-th projection
323	  \((s_\bullet) \mapsto s_i\) is the isomorphism \(\rho_i\) (with inverse extending a chart
324	  section by the overlap relation).
325	  The overlap compatibility is the defining relation of the compatible families: over
326	  \(V_{ij}\) the components \(s_i, s_j\) are related by \(g_{ij}\), which is precisely the
327	  statement that \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\) differ by \(g_{ij}\) after transport to
328	  \(V_{ij}\); the triple-overlap coherence needed for this to be consistent is (C2),
329	  whose transports are aligned by \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} and
330	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}.
331	\end{proof}
332	
333	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the glued sheaf up to canonical isomorphism]
334	  \label{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}
335	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue_unique}
336	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
337	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
338	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
339	  Suppose \(\mathcal{M}'\) is another sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules equipped with
340	  isomorphisms \(\rho'_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
341	  compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps. Then there is a unique isomorphism
342	  \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) intertwining the restriction
343	  isomorphisms, i.e.\ with \(\rho'_i \circ \iota_i^{*}(\cdot) = \rho_i\) for every \(i\).
344	\end{lemma}
345	\begin{proof}
346	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
347	  A morphism of sheaves of modules on \(X\) is determined by, and may be assembled from,
348	  its restrictions to the members of the open cover \(\{\iota_i\}\), agreeing on the
349	  overlaps -- the locality and gluing of sections
350	  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom. The
351	  intertwining condition forces the restriction over \(U_i\) to be
352	  \((\rho'_i)^{-1} \circ \rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}'\);
353	  these local isomorphisms agree on each overlap \(V_{ij}\) because both
354	  \((\rho_i)\) and \((\rho'_i)\) are compatible with the \emph{same} transition family
355	  \((g_{ij})\) (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}). By locality they glue to a
356	  unique global isomorphism \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) with the
357	  required property; uniqueness is the separation half of the same gluing.
358	\end{proof}
359	
360	\begin{definition}[Descent of morphisms along the cover]
361	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
362	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueHom}
363	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
364	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
365	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
366	  Let \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) be two glued data
367	  over the same glue datum \(D\), with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\) and
368	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i, \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i\). Given a family
369	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
370	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms on every overlap (i.e.\ \(h_{ij}\) intertwines
371	  the pullbacks of \(\phi_i\) and \(\phi_j\) over \(V_{ij}\)), there is a unique morphism
372	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) with
373	  \[
374	    \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi \;=\; \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i
375	    \qquad \text{for every } i.
376	  \]
377	\end{definition}
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
380	  On each chart the prescribed identity defines a local morphism
381	  \((\rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i)^{-1} \circ \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i :
382	  \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{N}\). The commutation of the \(\phi_i\)
383	  with the transition isomorphisms \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\), together with the overlap
384	  compatibility of the \(\rho\)-families (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}), makes
385	  these local morphisms agree on every overlap \(V_{ij}\). By the locality and gluing of
386	  sections (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom they
387	  promote to a unique global morphism \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) restricting
388	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the
389	  cover.
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
393	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
394	
395	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
396	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
397	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
398	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
399	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
400	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
401	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
402	
403	\begin{lemma}\leanok
404	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
405	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
407	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
408	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
409	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
410	\end{lemma}
411	
412	\begin{proof}
413	
414	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
415	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
416	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
417	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
418	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
419	\end{proof}
420	
421	\begin{lemma}\leanok
422	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
423	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
425	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
426	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
427	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
428	  \[
429	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
430	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
431	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
432	  \]
433	\end{lemma}
434	
435	\begin{proof}
436	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
437	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
438	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
439	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
440	\end{proof}
441	
442	\begin{lemma}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Free-pullback comparison is natural in the base morphism]
446	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
448	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
449	  Let \(\varphi, \psi : T' \to T\) be morphisms with \(\varphi = \psi\) and \(I\) an index
450	  type. The two free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(\varphi\) and
451	  \(\psi\) agree after transporting the differing pullback sources by the canonical
452	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\): the composite of that transport with
453	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)\) equals \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)\).
454	\end{lemma}
455	\begin{proof}
456	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
457	  Substituting the equality \(\varphi = \psi\) collapses the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) to the
458	  identity and the two comparisons coincide.
459	\end{proof}
460	
461	\begin{lemma}
462	
463	\leanok
464	[Iso-level free-pullback cancellation]
465	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
466	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
467	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
468	  For equal base morphisms \(\varphi = \psi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), the composite
469	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)^{-1}\) is the
470	  \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) transport between the (differing) pullback sources of the free sheaf
471	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\). Stated and proved generically in the base morphisms, so its
472	  use never forces evaluation of a concrete immersion; this is the load-bearing replacement
473	  for the cast chain in the self-identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}.
474	\end{lemma}
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
477	  Substituting \(\varphi = \psi\) turns the composite into
478	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)^{-1} =
479	  \mathrm{id}\), which is the \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) of the now-trivial source equality.
480	\end{proof}
481	
482	\begin{lemma}\leanok
483	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
484	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
485	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
486	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
487	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
488	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
489	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
490	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
491	\end{lemma}
492	
493	\begin{proof}
494	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
495	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
496	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
497	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
498	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
499	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
500	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
501	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
502	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
503	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
504	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
505	\end{proof}
506	
507	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
508	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
509	
510	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
511	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
512	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
513	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
514	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
515	
516	\begin{definition}\leanok
517	[Global section of the unit module]
518	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
519	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
520	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
521	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
522	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
523	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
524	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
525	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
526	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
527	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
528	  global function.
529	\end{definition}
530	
531	\begin{definition}\leanok
532	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
533	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
535	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
536	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
537	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
538	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
539	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
540	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
541	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
542	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
543	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
544	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
545	\end{definition}
546	
547	\begin{lemma}\leanok
548	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
549	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
550	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
551	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
552	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
553	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
554	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
555	\end{lemma}
556	
557	\begin{proof}
558	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
559	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
560	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
561	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
562	\end{proof}
563	
564	\begin{lemma}\leanok
565	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
566	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
567	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
568	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
569	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
570	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
571	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
572	\end{lemma}
573	
574	\begin{proof}
575	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
576	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
577	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
578	\end{proof}
579	
580	\begin{lemma}\leanok
581	[Composition of scalar endomorphisms is multiplication]
582	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}
583	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_comp}
584	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
585	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the scalar endomorphisms compose by
586	  multiplication: \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) \circ \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) =
587	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a\,b)\).
588	\end{lemma}
589	\begin{proof}
590	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
591	  Under the identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the
592	  scalar endomorphisms act as multiplication by \(a\) and by \(b\); their composite
593	  is multiplication by \(a\,b\), because the section ring is commutative and
594	  restriction maps are ring homomorphisms.
595	\end{proof}
596	
597	\begin{lemma}\leanok
598	[Scalar endomorphism is additive]
599	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
600	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_add}
601	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
602	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) one has \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a + b) =
603	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) + \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b)\).
604	\end{lemma}
605	\begin{proof}
606	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
607	  Multiplication by \(a + b\) equals multiplication by \(a\) plus multiplication by
608	  \(b\) on every section, since the restriction maps of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) are additive;
609	  under \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) this is the stated additivity.
610	\end{proof}
611	
612	\begin{lemma}
613	
614	\leanok
615	[Scalar endomorphism of a finite sum]
616	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum}
617	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_sum}
618	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
619	  For a finite index set \(s\) and a family \(f : \iota \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\),
620	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_{i \in s} f_i\bigr) = \sum_{i \in s} \mathrm{scalarEnd}(f_i)\).
621	\end{lemma}
622	\begin{proof}
623	  \uses{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
624	  Induction on the finite set \(s\): the empty case is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
625	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}) and the insertion step is additivity
626	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}).
627	\end{proof}
628	
629	\emph{Infrastructure: matrix automorphisms of the free sheaf.} To realise the
630	\(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transitions one must promote an invertible \(d \times d\)
631	matrix of global functions to an \emph{automorphism} of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf
632	\(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\), exactly as the chart quotient \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}
633	realises the universal matrix. The next blocks assemble this matrix-to-morphism
634	functor and record its two structural identities -- that it carries matrix
635	multiplication to composition and the identity matrix to the identity -- which are
636	the algebraic heart of the bundle cocycle.
637	
638	\begin{definition}\leanok
639	[Matrix endomorphism of the free sheaf]
640	  \label{def:gr_matrixEnd}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd}
642	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
643	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
644	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a matrix of
645	  global functions. The \emph{matrix endomorphism} \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the
646	  endomorphism of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) whose
647	  \((p,q)\)-component, under the presentation of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as the
648	  finite biproduct of \(d\) copies of the unit module \(\mathbf{1}\), is the scalar
649	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,q})\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is
650	  assembled from these components by the universal property of the rank-\(d\)
651	  biproduct, exactly as the chart quotient \(u^I\) is assembled
652	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). This is the substitute for the (non-existent)
653	  Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) endomorphism of a free sheaf'' primitive.
654	\end{definition}
655	
656	\begin{lemma}\leanok
657	[Matrix endomorphism carries multiplication to composition]
658	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
659	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_comp}
660	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
661	  For \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) the matrix
662	  endomorphisms compose according to the matrix product, with the order reversed by
663	  the contravariance of the component indexing:
664	  \[
665	    \mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
666	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N \cdot M).
667	  \]
668	\end{lemma}
669	\begin{proof}
670	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
671	  The composition of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again biproduct-assembled,
672	  with \((i,q)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(p\) of the composites of
673	  components, \(\sum_p \mathrm{component}_{i,p} \circ \mathrm{component}_{p,q}\) (the
674	  categorical matrix product over the biproduct). Each composite of scalar
675	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the product,
676	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(ab)\)
677	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}), and a finite sum of scalar endomorphisms is the
678	  scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence the \((i,q)\)-component
679	  of the composite is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_p M_{q,p} N_{p,i}\bigr) =
680	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((N\cdot M)_{q,i}\bigr)\), which is precisely the
681	  \((i,q)\)-component of \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N\cdot M)\). The reversal of the factor
682	  order is the contravariance of the column/component indexing.
683	\end{proof}
684	
685	\begin{lemma}\leanok
686	[Matrix endomorphism of the identity is the identity]
687	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
688	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_one}
689	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
690	  The matrix endomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism of
691	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\): \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1_{d\times d}) =
692	  \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
693	\end{lemma}
694	\begin{proof}
695	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
696	  The diagonal components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\)
697	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the off-diagonal components are
698	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the morphism with this
699	  component matrix is the identity of the biproduct.
700	\end{proof}
701	
702	\begin{definition}\leanok
703	[Free-sheaf automorphism of an invertible matrix]
704	  \label{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso}
706	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
707	  Let \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) be mutually
708	  inverse, \(M N = 1\) and \(N M = 1\). The \emph{matrix automorphism}
709	  \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is the isomorphism of free sheaves
710	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) with forward map
711	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) and inverse map \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)\); the two
712	  composites are identities by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp} (turning the products
713	  \(NM\) and \(MN\) into the relevant compositions) and \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
714	  Its forward map is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) by construction
715	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}).
716	\end{definition}
717	
718	\begin{lemma}\leanok
719	[Forward map of the matrix automorphism]
720	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}
721	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_hom}
722	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
723	  The forward map of \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
724	\end{lemma}
725	\begin{proof}
726	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
727	  Immediate from the definition \cref{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}, whose forward field is
728	  literally \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\begin{lemma}\leanok
732	[Matrix automorphisms compose multiplicatively]
733	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul}
734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_mul}
735	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
736	  Let \(A, A', B, B'\) be \(d \times d\) matrices of global functions on \(S\) with
737	  \(A A' = A' A = 1\), \(B B' = B' B = 1\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')\)
738	  and \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')\) are automorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\).
739	  Their forward maps compose to the forward map of the product automorphism, with the
740	  order reversed by the component contravariance:
741	  \[
742	    \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')_{\mathrm{hom}}
743	      \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')_{\mathrm{hom}}
744	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B \cdot A).
745	  \]
746	  Equivalently, composition of matrix automorphisms realises matrix multiplication of
747	  the underlying matrices. This is the linkage that turns the matrix-level cocycle
748	  identity into a composition identity of sheaf-of-module isomorphisms.
749	\end{lemma}
750	\begin{proof}
751	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
752	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom} the two forward maps are
753	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\) and \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B)\); their composite is
754	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B) = \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B\cdot A)\) by
755	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}.

769	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$

774	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
775	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
776	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
777	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
778	  \[
779	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
780	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}

782	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
783	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
784	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
785	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
786	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
787	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
788	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
789	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
790	
791	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
792	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
793	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
794	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
795	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
796	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification

798	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
799	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
800	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
801	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
802	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
803	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
804	  free sheaves'' primitive.

842	\begin{lemma}\leanok
843	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
844	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
845	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
846	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
847	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
848	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
849	\end{lemma}
850	
851	\begin{proof}
852	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
853	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
854	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
855	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
856	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
857	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
858	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
859	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
860	  \[
861	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
862	  \]
863	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
864	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
865	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
866	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
867	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
868	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
869	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
870	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
871	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
872	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
873	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
874	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
875	\end{proof}

877	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
878	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
879	
880	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
881	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
882	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
883	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
884	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
885	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
886	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
887	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
888	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
889	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
890	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
891	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.

1082	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\). The pullback functor \(p^{*}\) is a left adjoint, hence
1083	  additive and biproduct-preserving; through the coproduct comparison underlying the
1084	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation
1085	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) and the assembled morphism to the
1086	  morphism reassembled from the pulled-back components, the reassociation of the iterated
1087	  pullbacks being \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the
1088	  pulled-back scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the
1089	  comparison-conjugate of \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp}M_{k,\ell}) =
1090	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the biproduct, the conjugating
1091	  one-element comparisons combine into the single free comparison \(Q\), giving
1092	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)) = Q^{-1}\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q\).
1093	\end{proof}
