1095	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1096	[Affine global-sections comorphism is the inducing ring homomorphism]
1097	  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
1098	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
1099	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1100	  Let \(\varphi : A \to B\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
1101	  \(\Spec\varphi : \Spec B \to \Spec A\) the induced morphism of affine schemes. Under the
1102	  natural identification of the global sections of an affine scheme with its coordinate ring
1103	  -- the counit \(\Gamma(\Spec A, \mathcal O) \xrightarrow{\sim} A\) of the
1104	  \(\Gamma \dashv \Spec\) adjunction, and likewise for \(B\) -- the global-sections base-change
1105	  comorphism
1106	  \[
1107	    (\Spec\varphi)^{\sharp} : \Gamma(\Spec A, \mathcal O) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\Spec B, \mathcal O)
1108	  \]
1109	  is identified with \(\varphi\) itself; that is, the square relating
1110	  \((\Spec\varphi)^{\sharp}\) to \(\varphi\) through the two counit isomorphisms commutes.
1111	\end{lemma}
1112	\begin{proof}
1113	  This is the naturality of the counit isomorphism \(\Gamma \circ \Spec \cong \mathrm{id}\) of
1114	  the global-sections / spectrum adjunction: for any ring homomorphism \(\varphi\) the comorphism
1115	  of \(\Spec\varphi\) on global sections is, after transport through the natural counit
1116	  isomorphisms at \(A\) and \(B\), the homomorphism \(\varphi\). No Grassmannian-specific data
1117	  enters; the statement is the affine-scheme naturality square applied to a single ring map.
1118	\end{proof}

1370	The tautological quotient \(u\) is assembled from the chart quotients \(u^I\) by the
1371	section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}; its one remaining content is the
1372	\emph{overlap-compatibility} of the family \((u^I)\) with the bundle gluing
1373	\((g_{I,J})\). The next four blocks isolate that compatibility: a rectangular analogue of
1374	the square matrix-endomorphism API (\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd},
1375	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}) for the
1376	\(r \to d\) chart quotient (\cref{def:gr_matrixEndRect},
1377	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}), the
1378	adjunction transposition that turns the descent condition into a pullback-level identity
1379	(\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}), and the matrix-core assembly
1380	that closes it (\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}).

1382	\subsection{Matrix-calculus toolbox}
1383	
1384	The chart-cover lemma (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}) and the overlap compatibility of
1385	\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient} both rest on a small calculus of \emph{rectangular} matrix
1386	homomorphisms of free sheaves, generalising the square API
1387	(\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}). The
1388	blocks below collect that toolbox: the bridge to the square case, the identity and
1389	composition laws, injectivity of the matrix presentation, the column-restriction law, the
1390	affine splitting of epimorphisms of free sheaves and the resulting matrix right inverse, the
1391	field-level minor extraction, and the presented-matrix machinery that packages a quotient as
1392	a matrix along a morphism and proves Nitsure's change-of-basis identity. They are
1393	project-bespoke infrastructure.
1394	
1395	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1396	[Square and rectangular matrix homomorphisms coincide]
1397	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1398	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1399	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1400	  For a square matrix \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), the
1401	  square and rectangular matrix homomorphisms agree:
1402	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\).
1403	\end{lemma}
1404	\begin{proof}
1405	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1406	  Both are the morphism assembled from the same biproduct components
1407	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\) over the index set \(\{1,\dots,d\}\); the two
1408	  constructions are literally the same, so the equality holds by definition.
1409	\end{proof}

1416	  The rectangular homomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism:

1425	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1426	[Fully rectangular composition law]
1427	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect}
1428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_comp_rect}
1429	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum,
1430	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_add, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}
1431	  For \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{e\times n}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\) and
1432	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), composing two
1433	  rectangular homomorphisms realises the matrix product, the order reversed by the
1434	  contravariant component indexing exactly as in \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}: the
1435	  composite
1436	  \[
1437	    \mathcal{O}_S^{\,n} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(A)} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,e}
1438	      \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(B)} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}
1439	  \]
1440	  equals \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(B \cdot A)\) (the \(d \times n\) product).
1441	\end{lemma}
1442	\begin{proof}
1443	  \uses{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
1444	  The same biproduct-extensionality computation as \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}, with the
1445	  middle biproduct indexed by \(\{1,\dots,e\}\). The \((k,\ell)\)-component of the composite
1446	  is \(\sum_m \mathrm{scalarEnd}(B_{k,m})\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(A_{m,\ell})\); each composite
1447	  is the scalar endomorphism of the product (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}) and a finite sum
1448	  of scalar endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum},
1449	  \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}), so the component is
1450	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}((B\cdot A)_{k,\ell})\), the \((k,\ell)\)-component of
1451	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(B\cdot A)\).
1452	\end{proof}

1454	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1455	[Uniqueness of the presenting matrix]
1456	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
1457	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_injective}
1458	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_scalarEnd}
1459	  The matrix presentation is injective: if
1460	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M) = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N)\) for
1461	  \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), then \(M = N\).
1462	\end{lemma}
1463	\begin{proof}
1464	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
1465	  Extract the \((p,j)\)-entry by pre-composing with the free-basis inclusion of the
1466	  \(j\)-th summand and post-composing with the projection onto the \(p\)-th summand: this
1467	  isolates the scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,j})\) from
1468	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\). Equal homomorphisms therefore give
1469	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,j}) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(N_{p,j})\); and \(a \mapsto
1470	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) is injective (its inverse reads off the underlying section), whence
1471	  \(M_{p,j} = N_{p,j}\) for all \(p, j\).
1472	\end{proof}

1474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1475	[Column restriction against the index inclusion]
1476	  \label{lem:gr_freeMap_matrixEndRect}
1477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.freeMap_matrixEndRect}
1478	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1479	  For an index map \(g : \{1,\dots,e\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) and
1480	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), pre-composing the
1481	  rectangular homomorphism with the free index inclusion
1482	  \(\mathrm{freeMap}(g) : \mathcal{O}_S^{\,e} \to \mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}\) restricts to the
1483	  \(g\)-columns:
1484	  \[
1485	    \mathrm{freeMap}(g) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)
1486	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEndRect}\bigl(M_{\bullet,\, g}\bigr),
1487	  \]
1488	  where \(M_{\bullet,\, g}\) is the \(d \times e\) submatrix with \(\ell\)-th column the
1489	  \(g(\ell)\)-th column of \(M\).
1490	\end{lemma}
1491	\begin{proof}
1492	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1493	  Both sides agree on each free generator \(j\): the inclusion sends it to the \(g(j)\)-th
1494	  generator, and reading off the \((p,j)\)-entry on either side gives \(M_{p, g(j)}\). Cofan
1495	  extensionality over the \(e\) generators concludes.
1496	\end{proof}

1498	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1499	[Epimorphisms of free sheaves split over an affine]
1500	  \label{lem:gr_exists_section_of_epi_free_spec}
1501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_section_of_epi_free_spec}
1502	  On an affine base \(\Spec R\), every epimorphism
1503	  \(\psi : \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,d}\) of
1504	  free sheaves splits: there is a section
1505	  \(\Phi : \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,r}\) with
1506	  \(\psi \circ \Phi = \mathrm{id}\).
1507	\end{lemma}
1508	\begin{proof}
1509	  Under the fully faithful additive functor \(\mathrm{Mod}_R \to (\Spec R)\text{-Mod}\)
1510	  taking a module to its associated quasi-coherent sheaf, the free sheaves correspond to the
1511	  free modules \(R^{\,r}, R^{\,d}\) and \(\psi\) to a surjection of modules. A free module is
1512	  projective, so this surjection admits a module-level section; transporting that section
1513	  back through full faithfulness produces the required \(\Phi\).
1514	\end{proof}

1516	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1517	[Matrix right inverse over \(\Spec R\)]
1518	  \label{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec}
1519	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec}

1521	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1522	  For \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(\Spec R, \mathcal{O}))\) with
1523	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) an epimorphism, the matrix admits a right inverse: there is
1524	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(\Gamma(\Spec R, \mathcal{O}))\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\).
1525	\end{lemma}
1526	\begin{proof}
1527	  \uses{lem:gr_exists_section_of_epi_free_spec, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect,
1528	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1529	  Split the epimorphism by \cref{lem:gr_exists_section_of_epi_free_spec} and read its section
1530	  off as a matrix \(G\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(G)\) is the section. The composition
1531	  law (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect}) gives \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M\cdot G)\) as the
1532	  composite of section and epimorphism, which is the identity; with
1533	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}) and injectivity
1534	  of the presentation (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}) this forces \(M \cdot G = 1\).
1535	\end{proof}

1557	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1558	[Field-level minor extraction]
1559	  \label{lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}
1560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_isUnit_submatrix}
1561	  Let \(K\) be a field, \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(K)\) and
1562	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(K)\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\). Then some size-\(d\)
1563	  column-index set \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) gives an invertible minor: the \(d \times d\)
1564	  submatrix \(M_I\) of the \(I\)-columns (in their natural order) is a unit.
1565	\end{lemma}
1566	\begin{proof}
1567	  The right inverse makes the column map \(K^{\,r} \to K^{\,d}\) surjective, so the columns of
1568	  \(M\) span \(K^{\,d}\). A spanning family contains a basis; choosing \(d\) columns forming a
1569	  basis, the corresponding \(d \times d\) submatrix has linearly independent columns and is
1570	  therefore invertible.
1571	\end{proof}

1709	\leanok
1710	[Rectangular matrix homomorphism of free sheaves]
1711	  \label{def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1712	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect}
1713	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
1714	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d, r \in \mathbb{N}\), and
1715	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a rectangular matrix
1716	  of global functions. The \emph{rectangular matrix homomorphism}
1717	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) is the morphism of free sheaves
1718	  \[
1719	    \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M) \;:\; \mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}
1720	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}
1721	  \]
1722	  whose \((k,\ell)\)-component -- \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\) an output index,
1723	  \(\ell \in \{1,\dots,r\}\) an input index -- under the presentation of
1724	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}\) and \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as finite biproducts of copies of the
1725	  unit module \(\mathbf 1\), is the scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\)
1726	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is assembled from these components by the universal property
1727	  of the two biproducts, exactly as the square \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\)
1728	  (\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}) and the chart quotient (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). When
1729	  \(r = d\) it specialises to \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\); and the chart quotient \(u^I\)
1730	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is by construction \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(X^I)\) of the
1731	  universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}).
1732	\end{definition}

1734	\begin{lemma}
1735	
1736	\leanok
1737	[Rectangular matrix homomorphism is natural under pullback]
1738	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}
1739	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_pullback}
1740	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback,
1741	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1742	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and
1743	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), with entrywise
1744	  base-changed matrix \(p^{\sharp}M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(T,\mathcal{O}_T))\).
1745	  Write \(Q_r : p^{*}\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) and
1746	  \(Q_d : p^{*}\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) for the
1747	  free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) at the index sets
1748	  \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Then the pullback of
1749	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) is conjugate, through \(Q_r\) and \(Q_d\), to the
1750	  rectangular homomorphism of the base-changed matrix:
1751	  \[
1752	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\bigr)
1753	      \;=\; Q_d^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(p^{\sharp}M) \,\circ\, Q_r .
1754	  \]
1755	\end{lemma}
1756	\begin{proof}
1757	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1758	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1759	  Identical to the square case \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}, with the source and target
1760	  biproducts now indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\) respectively. The
1761	  pullback functor \(p^{*}\), a left adjoint, is additive and biproduct-preserving; through
1762	  the coproduct comparisons underlying the free-pullback isomorphisms
1763	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation of
1764	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\)) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
1765	  (resp. \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\)), the reassociation of the iterated pullbacks being
1766	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the pulled-back
1767	  scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the comparison-conjugate of
1768	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the two biproducts, the
1769	  conjugating one-element comparisons combine into \(Q_r\) on the source and \(Q_d\) on the
1770	  target, giving \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)) = Q_d^{-1}\circ
1771	  \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q_r\).
1772	\end{proof}
1773	
1774	\begin{lemma}
1775	
1776	\leanok
1777	[Square-after-rectangular composition law]
1778	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}
1779	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_comp}
1780	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add,
1781	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
1782	  For \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\) and
1783	  \(N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), post-composing the
1784	  rectangular homomorphism with a square one realises the matrix product, with the order
1785	  reversed by the contravariance of the component indexing exactly as in
1786	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}:
1787	  \[
1788	    \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
1789	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N \cdot M),
1790	  \]
1791	  the composite \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)}
1792	  \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) (here
1793	  \(N \cdot M\) is the \(d \times r\) product).
1794	\end{lemma}
1795	\begin{proof}
1796	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add,
1797	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
1798	  The same biproduct-extensionality computation as \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}, the only
1799	  change being that the leftmost (input) biproduct is indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) rather
1800	  than \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). The composite of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again
1801	  biproduct-assembled, with \((k,\ell)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(m\) of the
1802	  composites of components, \(\sum_m \mathrm{scalarEnd}(N_{k,m}) \circ
1803	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{m,\ell})\). Each composite of scalar endomorphisms is the scalar
1804	  endomorphism of the product (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}) and a finite sum of scalar
1805	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence
1806	  that component is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(\sum_m N_{k,m} M_{m,\ell}) =
1807	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((N\cdot M)_{k,\ell})\), the \((k,\ell)\)-component of
1808	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N\cdot M)\).
1809	\end{proof}
1810	
1811	\begin{lemma}
1812	
1813	\leanok
1814	[Adjunction transpose of the chart-overlap condition]
1815	  \label{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}
1816	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}
1817	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_bundleTransition, def:gr_modules_glueHom,
1818	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app,
1819	    lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1820	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1821	  Fix size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J\) and let \(f_{IJ} : U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\),
1822	  \(t_{IJ} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\) be the overlap immersion and transition
1823	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}). The per-chart components \(u^I\) of the tautological quotient
1824	  are the adjoint transposes, along the chart immersions, of the chart quotients
1825	  \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient} (precomposed with the free-pullback comparison). Their
1826	  descent compatibility -- the hypothesis the gluing primitive \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
1827	  consumes, namely that the bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_bundleTransition})
1828	  intertwines the pullbacks of \(u^I\) and \(u^J\) over \(U^I_J\) -- is equivalent, under the
1829	  pullback--pushforward adjunction of the overlap immersion, to the pullback-level identity
1830	  of morphisms \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\)
1831	  \[
1832	    g_{I,J} \,\circ\, f_{IJ}^{*}\,u^I
1833	      \;=\; (t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI})^{*}\,u^J,
1834	  \]
1835	  understood through the free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) on
1836	  source and target.
1837	\end{lemma}
1838	\begin{proof}
1839	  \uses{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app, lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib,
1840	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1841	  The components are produced by applying the adjunction hom-equivalence to the chart
1842	  quotients; the descent condition of \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} is stated on those
1843	  transposes. Transposing back across the adjunction is the naturality of the conjugate
1844	  (mate) construction against the hom-equivalences, \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app},
1845	  together with the compatibility of the conjugate with the iterated-pullback comparison
1846	  \cref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} reassociating the two pullbacks
1847	  \(f_{IJ}^{*}\) and \((t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*}\) via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The
1848	  free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} re-present both sides as morphisms
1849	  of the trivial bundles \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\), under
1850	  which the transposed condition becomes the displayed pullback-level identity.
1851	\end{proof}
1852	
1853	\begin{lemma}
1854	
1855	\leanok
1856	[Overlap compatibility of the tautological quotient]
1857	  \label{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}
1858	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient_overlap}
1859	  \uses{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback,
1860	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp, def:gr_bundleTransition, def:gr_chart_quotient,
1861	    def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap,
1862	    lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport,
1863	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}

1865	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) satisfy the overlap-compatibility condition required by the
1866	  section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} for the bundle gluing \((g_{I,J})\):
1867	  the pullback-level identity \(g_{I,J} \circ f_{IJ}^{*} u^I = (t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*} u^J\)
1868	  of \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose} holds on each overlap \(U^I_J\).

1870	\begin{proof}
1871	  \uses{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback,
1872	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp, def:gr_bundleTransition,
1873	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq,
1874	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}
1875	  By \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose} it suffices to prove the
1876	  pullback-level identity \(g_{I,J} \circ f_{IJ}^{*} u^I = (t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*} u^J\) on
1877	  \(U^I_J\). Both sides are morphisms of trivial bundles \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,r} \to
1878	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\); write each as \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) of a \(d \times r\)
1879	  matrix over the overlap ring \(R^I_J\) and compare matrices.
1880	
1881	  Since \(u^I = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(X^I)\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
1882	  base-change naturality \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback} identifies \(f_{IJ}^{*} u^I\),
1883	  through the free-pullback comparisons, with \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) of the localised
1884	  matrix \(X^I\) over \(R^I_J\) (the global-sections base change is the away-localisation
1885	  \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}). The bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\) is
1886	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\) of the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)
1887	  (\cref{def:gr_bundleTransition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}); hence by the
1888	  square-after-rectangular composition law \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp},
1889	  \[
1890	    g_{I,J} \circ f_{IJ}^{*} u^I
1891	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEndRect}\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} \cdot X^I\bigr).
1892	  \]
1893	  The transition image matrix identity \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
1894	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}, transported across the overlap by
1895	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}) turns the right-hand matrix into \(X^J\). Symmetrically,
1896	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}_J\) of \(X^J\) is, by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback} along
1897	  \(t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI}\), the comparison form of \((t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*} u^J\). Therefore
1898	  the two sides agree, which is the required overlap compatibility.
1899	\end{proof}
1900	
1901	\begin{definition}

1903	\leanok
1904	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]

1912	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1913	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).

1925	  \]

1928	  module level this overlap compatibility -- the equalising condition consumed by the
1929	  section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} -- is exactly

1938	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1939	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1940	  \]
1941	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1942	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1943	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1944	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1945	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1946	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
1947	\end{definition}
1948	
1949	\section{The functor of points}
1950	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1951	
1952	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1953	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1954	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1955	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1956	identities that make that action functorial.
1957	
1958	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1959	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1960	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1961	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1962	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1963	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1964	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1965	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1966	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1967	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1968	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1969	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1970	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1971	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1972	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1973	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1974	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1975	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1976	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1977	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1978	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1979	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1980	  functor's value at \(T\).
1981	
1982	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1983	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1984	  \[
1985	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1986	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1987	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1988	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1989	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1990	  \]
1991	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1992	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1993	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1994	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1995	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1996	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1997	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1998	  map of equivalence classes.
1999	\end{definition}
2000	
2001	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
2002	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
2003	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
2004	
2005	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2006	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
2007	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
2008	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
2009	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
2010	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
2011	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
2012	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
2013	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
2014	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
2015	\end{lemma}
2016	
2017	\begin{proof}
2018	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
2019	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
2020	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
2021	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
2022	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
2023	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
2024	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
2025	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
2026	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
2027	\end{proof}
2028	
2029	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2030	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
2031	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
2032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
2033	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
2034	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
2035	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
2036	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
2037	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
2038	\end{lemma}
2039	
2040	\begin{proof}
2041	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
2042	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
2043	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
2044	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
2045	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
2046	\end{proof}
2047	
2048	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2049	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
2050	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
2051	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
2052	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
2053	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
2054	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
2055	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
2056	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
2057	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
2058	\end{lemma}
2059	
2060	\begin{proof}
2061	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
2062	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
2063	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
2064	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
2065	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
2066	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
2067	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
2068	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
2069	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
2070	  well-defined.
2071	\end{proof}
2072	
2073	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2074	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
2075	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
2076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
2077	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
2078	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
2079	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
2080	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
2081	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
2082	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
2083	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
2084	\end{lemma}
2085	
2086	\begin{proof}
2087	
2088	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
2089	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
2090	  hom-set bijections.
2091	\end{proof}
2092	
2093	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2094	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
2095	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
2096	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
2097	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
2098	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
2099	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
2100	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
2101	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
2102	\end{lemma}
2103	
2104	\begin{proof}
2105	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
2106	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
2107	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
2108	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf
2109	  tracked through each pullback).
2110	\end{proof}
2111	
2112	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
2113	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
2114	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
2115	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
2116	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
2117	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
2118	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.

2120	\begin{definition}
2121	
2122	\leanok
2123	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
2124	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
2125	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
2126	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
2127	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
2128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2129	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
2130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
2131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2132	  % "represents the
2133	  %  contravariant functor
2134	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
2135	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
2136	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2137	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
2138	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
2139	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
2140	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
2141	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
2142	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
2143	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
2144	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
2145	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
2146	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
2147	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2148	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
2149	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
2150	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
2151	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
2152	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
2153	  \[
2154	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
2155	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
2156	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
2157	  \]
2158	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
2159	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
2160	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
2161	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
2162	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
2163	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
2164	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
2165	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
2166	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
2167	\end{definition}
2168	
2169	\section{The universal property}
2170	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
2171	
2172	The representability theorem \cref{thm:grassmannian_universal_property} has two halves:
2173	the forward map \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u\rangle\)
2174	and its inverse, which manufactures a morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) out of a rank-\(d\)
2175	quotient \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\). The forward map needs the tautological
2176	quotient to actually be a quotient -- that \(u\) is an epimorphism
2177	(\cref{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}). The inverse is built Zariski-locally and glued: for
2178	each size-\(d\) subset \(I\) one isolates the open locus \(T_I \subseteq T\) where the
2179	\(I\)-indexed columns of \(q\) form a basis of \(\mathcal{F}\), classifies \(T_I \to U^I\)
2180	there, and glues over the cover \(\{T_I\}\). The blocks of this section supply the four
2181	ingredients of that inverse: the iso-locus of a sheaf morphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}) and
2182	the fact that a morphism becomes invertible after restriction to its iso-locus
2183	(\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}); the chart locus \(T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus})
2184	and the proof that the chart loci cover \(T\) (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}); and the
2185	glued classifying morphism itself (\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient}).
2186	
2187	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2188	[The tautological quotient is an epimorphism]
2189	  \label{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}
2190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient_epi}
2191	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
2192	  The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
2193	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}) is an epimorphism of
2194	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-modules.
2195	\end{lemma}
2196	\begin{proof}
2197	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
2198	  Being an epimorphism of sheaves of modules is local on the base: a morphism is epi
2199	  precisely when its restriction to every member of an open cover is epi, since the
2200	  cokernel is computed chartwise and a sheaf vanishing on a cover vanishes. The charts
2201	  \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and over \(U^I\) the tautological quotient,
2202	  transported through the trivialisation \(\mathcal{U}|_{U^I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
2203	  of \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}, is the chart quotient \(u^I :
2204	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}).
2205	  Each \(u^I\) is a split epimorphism: its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\),
2206	  so the coordinate inclusion \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow
2207	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) on the \(I\)-columns is a section of \(u^I\), whence \(u^I\) is
2208	  epi (\cref{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}). As \(u^I\) is epi on every chart, \(u\) is epi.
2209	\end{proof}
2210	
2211	\begin{definition}[The universal quotient sheaf restricts to the free sheaf on a chart]
2212	  \label{def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso}
2213	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient_restrictionIso}
2214	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:glueRestrictionIso,
2215	    def:gr_bundleTransitionData, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}
2216	  For each chart index \(I\), the restriction of the universal quotient sheaf
2217	  \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) to the \(I\)-chart along the
2218	  glue-data immersion \(\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is canonically isomorphic to
2219	  the free rank-\(d\) sheaf,
2220	  \[
2221	    \phi_I \;:\; \iota_I^{*}\mathcal{U}
2222	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}.
2223	  \]
2224	  It is the instantiation, at the Grassmannian glue data (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and
2225	  the bundle-transition cocycle \((g_{I,J})\) (\cref{def:gr_bundleTransitionData},
2226	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}, \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}), of the descent
2227	  restriction isomorphism (\cref{def:glueRestrictionIso}); its underlying morphism is the
2228	  adjoint transpose of the \(I\)-th descent-equaliser projection, an isomorphism because
2229	  the descent comparison morphism is invertible.
2230	\end{definition}
2231	
2232	\begin{lemma}[The universal quotient sheaf is locally free of rank \(d\)]
2233	  \label{lem:gr_universalQuotient_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
2234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
2235	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso,
2236	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp,
2237	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
2238	  The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
2239	  locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}).
2240	\end{lemma}
2241	\begin{proof}
2242	  \uses{def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso, def:modules_pullbackComp,
2243	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
2244	  The chart images \(\{\iota_I(U^I)\}\) form an open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), since
2245	  the glue-data immersions are jointly surjective (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). Over each
2246	  member, factor the immersion as \(\iota_I = e_I \circ j_I\) through the isomorphism
2247	  \(e_I : U^I \xrightarrow{\sim} \iota_I(U^I)\) onto its open image followed by the open
2248	  inclusion \(j_I\); transporting the chart restriction isomorphism
2249	  (\cref{def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso}) across this factorisation -- inverting
2250	  the parametrisation \(e_I\) through the pullback pseudofunctor comparison
2251	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and the free-pullback comparison
2252	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) -- exhibits the restriction
2253	  \(j_I^{*}\mathcal{U} \cong \mathcal{O}_{\iota_I(U^I)}^{\,d}\) as free of rank \(d\). As
2254	  the \(\iota_I(U^I)\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally free of
2255	  rank \(d\).
2256	\end{proof}

2258	\begin{definition}[The tautological point as a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2259	  \label{def:gr_tautologicalRankQuotient}
2260	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalRankQuotient}
2261	  \uses{def:gr_rankQuotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:tautological_quotient,
2262	    lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi, lem:gr_universalQuotient_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
2263	  The tautological pair \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle\) on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself,
2264	  packaged as a rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}\)
2265	  (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}): its sheaf is the universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)
2266	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}), its quotient map the tautological quotient
2267	  \(u\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), which is an epimorphism
2268	  (\cref{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}) onto a sheaf locally free of rank \(d\)
2269	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalQuotient_isLocallyFreeOfRank}). Pulling it back along a morphism
2270	  \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) realises the forward direction of the universal
2271	  property.

2274	\begin{definition}\leanok
2275	[Iso-locus of a morphism of sheaves of modules]
2276	  \label{def:isoLocus}
2277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isoLocus}
2278	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) a morphism of sheaves
2279	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The \emph{iso-locus} \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi) \subseteq X\)
2280	  is the open subscheme
2281	  \[
2282	    \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)
2283	      \;=\; \bigsqcup\,\bigl\{\, U \subseteq X \text{ open} \;:\;
2284	        \varphi|_U := (\iota_U^{*}\varphi) \text{ is an isomorphism} \,\bigr\},
2285	  \]
2286	  the supremum (union) of all opens \(U\) over which the pullback \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) of
2287	  \(\varphi\) to the open subscheme \(U\) is an isomorphism of sheaves of
2288	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules. Equivalently, a point \(t \in X\) lies in
2289	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) iff \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism over some open
2290	  neighbourhood of \(t\). It is the largest open on which \(\varphi\) is invertible; Mathlib
2291	  has no iso-locus primitive for morphisms of sheaves of modules, so it is set up directly
2292	  as this supremum.
2293	\end{definition}
2294	
2295	\begin{lemma}[Stalk-wise iso criterion]
2296	  \label{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2297	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso}
2298	  \mathlibok
2299	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2300	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves on a topological space \(X\) valued in a
2301	  concrete category whose forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms and preserves limits and
2302	  filtered colimits, and let \(f : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}\) be a morphism. If the
2303	  induced map on stalks \(f_t : \mathcal{F}_t \to \mathcal{G}_t\) is an isomorphism for every
2304	  point \(t \in X\), then \(f\) is an isomorphism.
2305	\end{lemma}
2306	
2307	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2308	[A morphism is invertible over its iso-locus]
2309	  \label{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2311	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2312	  For a morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of
2313	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the pullback of \(\varphi\) to the iso-locus
2314	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}),
2315	  \[
2316	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\varphi
2317	      \;:\;
2318	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{M}
2319	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2320	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{N},
2321	  \]
2322	  is an isomorphism.
2323	\end{lemma}
2324	\begin{proof}
2325	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2326	  Being an isomorphism is a stalk-local property: by the stalk-wise criterion
2327	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}) it suffices to show that the pulled-back
2328	  morphism induces an isomorphism on the stalk at every point \(t \in
2329	  \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\). By the definition of the iso-locus as a union of opens on
2330	  which \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}), each such \(t\) has an
2331	  open neighbourhood \(U \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) with \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) an
2332	  isomorphism; restriction of an isomorphism is an isomorphism, and pullback commutes with
2333	  taking stalks, so the stalk of the morphism at \(t\) coincides with the stalk of
2334	  \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) at \(t\), which is invertible. Hence every stalk map is an
2335	  isomorphism, and the criterion concludes that the global pullback over
2336	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism.
2337	\end{proof}

2339	\begin{definition}[Chart composite of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2340	  \label{def:gr_chartComposite}
2341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartComposite}
2342	  \uses{def:gr_rankQuotient}
2343	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
2344	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) and \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) a
2345	  size-\(d\) subset, enumerated in increasing order by the order isomorphism
2346	  \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\). The \emph{chart composite} is the morphism of
2347	  sheaves of modules
2348	  \[
2349	    s_I \circ q \;:\; \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2350	      \overset{\ s_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2351	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2352	  \]
2353	  the quotient \(q\) precomposed with the coordinate inclusion \(s_I\) of the
2354	  \(I\)-indexed factors of the trivial bundle. Over the locus where it is invertible the
2355	  \(I\)-columns of \(q\) form a basis of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2356	\end{definition}
2357	
2358	\begin{definition}\leanok
2359	[Chart locus of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2360	  \label{def:chartLocus}
2361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus}
2362	  \uses{def:isoLocus, def:gr_chartComposite, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2363	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
2364	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) on a scheme \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) and \(I \subseteq
2365	  \{1,\dots,r\}\) a size-\(d\) subset. The \emph{chart composite} is the morphism of sheaves
2366	  of modules
2367	  \[
2368	    s_I \circ q
2369	      \;:\;
2370	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2371	      \overset{\ s_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2372	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2373	  \]
2374	  obtained by precomposing the quotient \(q\) with the inclusion \(s_I\) of the
2375	  \(I\)-indexed coordinates of the trivial bundle. The \emph{chart locus}
2376	  \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I) \subseteq T\) is the iso-locus
2377	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}) of this chart composite:
2378	  \[
2379	    T_I \;=\; \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q),
2380	  \]
2381	  the largest open of \(T\) over which the \(I\)-columns of \(q\) form a basis of
2382	  \(\mathcal{F}\) -- i.e.\ over which \(s_I \circ q\) is an isomorphism. Over \(T_I\) the
2383	  quotient \(q\) is therefore presented, after trivialising \(\mathcal{F}\) by
2384	  \((s_I \circ q)^{-1}\), by a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-minor is the identity,
2385	  exactly the shape of the universal matrix \(X^I\) realising the chart quotient \(u^I\)
2386	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2387	\end{definition}

2389	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2390	[The chart loci cover \(T\)]
2391	  \label{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}
2392	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus_isOpenCover}
2393	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2394	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2395	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
2396	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L823-L828).
2397	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2398	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
2399	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
2400	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
2401	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
2402	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
2403	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
2404	  %  on $U^I_J$."
2405	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2406	  For a rank-\(d\) quotient \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
2407	  on \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), the family of chart loci
2408	  \(\{\,T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\,\}_{\#(I) = d}\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) is an open
2409	  cover of \(T\): \(\bigcup_{\#(I)=d} T_I = T\).
2410	\end{lemma}
2411	\begin{proof}
2412	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient, def:isoLocus, def:gr_matrixEndRect,
2413	    lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}
2414	  Each \(T_I\) is open, being an iso-locus (\cref{def:isoLocus}). It remains to show the
2415	  \(T_I\) cover \(T\), i.e.\ that every point \(t \in T\) lies in some \(T_I\). Fix \(t\) and
2416	  choose an \emph{affine} open \(W \ni t\) over which \(\mathcal{F}\) is trivialised,
2417	  \(\mathcal{F}|_W \cong \mathcal{O}_W^{\,d}\) (such \(W\) exists because \(\mathcal{F}\) is
2418	  locally free of rank \(d\)); over \(W\) the quotient \(q\) is presented by a \(d \times r\)
2419	  matrix \(M\) of sections of \(\mathcal{O}_W\), i.e.\ \(q|_W\) is conjugate to
2420	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) (\cref{def:gr_matrixEndRect}).
2421	
2422	  \emph{Global splitting over \(W\).} Because \(q\) is an epimorphism
2423	  (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), so is the presenting homomorphism
2424	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) of free sheaves on the affine \(W\). Over an affine base such
2425	  an epimorphism of free sheaves splits -- free modules are projective, so the epimorphism
2426	  admits a section -- and reading the section off as a matrix yields a right inverse
2427	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(\Gamma(W,\mathcal{O}_W))\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\)
2428	  (\cref{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect}). Thus \(q\) is globally split over
2429	  \(W\), with no recourse to stalks or Nakayama propagation.
2430	
2431	  \emph{Choosing the index set at \(t\).} Reduce the identity \(M \cdot G = 1\) modulo the
2432	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(t\): writing \(\kappa(t)\) for the residue field, the
2433	  images \(\bar M, \bar G\) over \(\kappa(t)\) still satisfy \(\bar M \cdot \bar G = 1\), so
2434	  \(\bar M\) is a right-invertible \(d \times r\) matrix over the field \(\kappa(t)\). A
2435	  right-invertible matrix over a field has an invertible \(d\)-column minor: its columns span
2436	  \(\kappa(t)^{d}\), a spanning family contains a basis, and the corresponding \(d\) columns
2437	  form an invertible \(d \times d\) submatrix (\cref{lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}). Pick
2438	  such a size-\(d\) index set \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\).
2439	
2440	  \emph{Cutting out the basic open.} Let \(P_I = \det(M_I)\) be the determinant of the
2441	  \(I\)-indexed \(d \times d\) minor of \(M\). By the previous step its residue at \(t\) is
2442	  nonzero, so \(P_I\) is a unit at \(t\); hence \(t\) lies in the basic open
2443	  \(W_{P_I} = \{P_I \ne 0\} \subseteq W\) where \(P_I\) is invertible. On \(W_{P_I}\) the
2444	  minor matrix \(M_I\) is invertible, so the chart composite \(s_I \circ q\) -- conjugate
2445	  there to \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M_I)\) -- restricts to an isomorphism, i.e.\
2446	  \(W_{P_I} \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q) = T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}).
2447	  Therefore \(t \in T_I\), and the chart loci cover \(T\).
2448	\end{proof}
2449	
2450	\begin{definition}[Chart matrix of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2451	  \label{def:gr_chartMatrix}
2452	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMatrix}
2453	  \uses{def:gr_chartComposite, def:chartLocus, lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map,
2454	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2455	  Over the chart locus \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) the chart
2456	  composite \(s_I \circ q\) pulls back to an isomorphism
2457	  (\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}). Trivialising \(\mathcal{F}|_{T_I}\) by its
2458	  inverse presents the restricted quotient as the conjugated free-sheaf homomorphism
2459	  \[
2460	    Q_r^{-1} \circ \iota_{T_I}^{*}q \circ (\iota_{T_I}^{*}(s_I \circ q))^{-1} \circ Q_d
2461	      \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d},
2462	  \]
2463	  with \(Q_r, Q_d\) the free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}). The
2464	  \emph{chart matrix} \(M^I = \mathrm{chartMatrix}(x, I) \in
2465	  \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I}))\) reads off this
2466	  homomorphism entrywise through the section-extraction \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\). It is
2467	  the special case of the presented matrix (\cref{def:gr_presentedMatrix}) along the
2468	  chart-locus inclusion; its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\).
2469	\end{definition}
2470	
2471	\begin{definition}[Chart morphism of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2472	  \label{def:gr_chartMorphism}
2473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMorphism}
2474	  \uses{def:gr_chartMatrix, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
2475	  The \emph{chart morphism} \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\) into the affine chart
2476	  \(U^I = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is the morphism classified,
2477	  through the \(\Gamma\)--\(\Spec\) adjunction, by the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism
2478	  \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending each variable
2479	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) to the
2480	  corresponding entry of the chart matrix \(M^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chartMatrix}). By
2481	  construction \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\), and hence \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q|_{T_I}\) in the
2482	  \(I\)-trivialisation.
2483	\end{definition}
2484	
2485	\subsection{Overlap compatibility of the chart morphisms}
2486	
2487	On the intersection \(P = T_I \times_T T_J\) of two chart loci both chart composites pull
2488	back to isomorphisms, so the pulled-back quotient is presented by both chart matrices. The
2489	blocks below assemble the transports comparing the two presentations and proving the two
2490	chart morphisms agree into the glued scheme: invertibility along a composite
2491	(\cref{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}), the presented matrix along a composite
2492	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}) and under an equality of base morphisms
2493	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_congr}), the chart matrix as a presented matrix
2494	(\cref{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}), its own minor
2495	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}), the realisation
2496	\(\mathrm{aeval}(M^I)(X^I) = M^I\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix}), the
2497	image-matrix transport (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom}), chart-morphism naturality
2498	(\cref{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism}), the glue-condition identity
2499	(\cref{lem:gr_chart_point_eq}), and finally the overlap compatibility itself
2500	(\cref{lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}).
2501	
2502	\begin{lemma}[Pullback-invertibility persists along precomposition]
2503	  \label{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}
2504	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_map_comp}
2505	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp}
2506	  For composable scheme morphisms \(p : W \to V\), \(a : V \to X\) and a morphism
2507	  \(c : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules: if the
2508	  pullback \(a^{*}c\) is an isomorphism, so is \((p \circ a)^{*}c\).
2509	\end{lemma}
2510	\begin{proof}
2511	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp}
2512	  Functoriality of pullback makes \(p^{*}(a^{*}c)\) an isomorphism; the pseudofunctor
2513	  comparison \((p \circ a)^{*} \cong p^{*}a^{*}\) (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp})
2514	  identifies \((p \circ a)^{*}c\) with \(p^{*}(a^{*}c)\), which is invertible.
2515	\end{proof}
2516	
2517	\begin{lemma}[The presented matrix depends only on the base morphism]
2518	  \label{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_congr}
2519	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix_congr}
2520	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2521	  If \(j = j'\) as morphisms \(P \to T\), then the presented matrices along \(j\) and \(j'\)
2522	  agree, \(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I) = \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j', I)\); the
2523	  choice of invertibility witness for the pulled-back chart composite is irrelevant.
2524	\end{lemma}
2525	\begin{proof}
2526	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2527	  Substitute the equality \(j = j'\); the invertibility hypotheses are propositions, hence
2528	  proof-irrelevant, and the two sides become definitionally equal.
2529	\end{proof}
2530	
2531	\begin{lemma}[The chart matrix is the presented matrix along the locus inclusion]
2532	  \label{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}
2533	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}
2534	  \uses{def:gr_chartMatrix, def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2535	  The chart matrix \(M^I = \mathrm{chartMatrix}(x, I)\) (\cref{def:gr_chartMatrix}) equals
2536	  the presented matrix (\cref{def:gr_presentedMatrix}) of \(x\) along the chart-locus
2537	  inclusion \(\iota_{T_I} : T_I \hookrightarrow T\):
2538	  \(\mathrm{chartMatrix}(x, I) = \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, \iota_{T_I}, I)\).
2539	\end{lemma}
2540	\begin{proof}
2541	  \uses{def:gr_chartMatrix, def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2542	  Both sides are the same \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\)-matrix of the same conjugated
2543	  free-sheaf homomorphism; the equality is definitional.
2544	\end{proof}
2545	
2546	\begin{lemma}[Transport of the presented matrix along a composite]
2547	  \label{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}
2548	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix_comp}
2549	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix,
2550	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2551	  Along composable \(p : W \to V\), \(a : V \to T\) on which the \(I\)-chart composite
2552	  pulls back to isomorphisms, the presented matrix after pulling back along \(p \circ a\)
2553	  is the entrywise restriction along \(p\) of the presented matrix after pulling back
2554	  along \(a\):
2555	  \[
2556	    \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, p \circ a, I)
2557	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, a, I)\bigr)\big|_{p},
2558	  \]
2559	  the matrix obtained by applying the restriction ring map \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_V) \to
2560	  \Gamma(W, \mathcal{O}_W)\) of \(p\) to every entry.
2561	\end{lemma}
2562	\begin{proof}
2563	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2564	  By injectivity of the rectangular matrix presentation
2565	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}) it suffices to compare after applying
2566	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\). The presentation identity
2567	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix}) expresses both sides as conjugated
2568	  pulled-back homomorphisms; the conjugation distributes over the composite \(p \circ a\)
2569	  through the pullback pseudofunctor comparisons, and the entrywise restriction along
2570	  \(p\) is exactly the pullback along \(p\) of the \(a\)-level homomorphism. Comparing the
2571	  two yields the claimed equality.
2572	\end{proof}
2573	
2574	\begin{lemma}[The own-minor of the presented matrix is the identity]
2575	  \label{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2577	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor,
2578	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2579	  Along any \(j\) on which the \(I\)-chart composite pulls back to an isomorphism, the
2580	  \(I\)-minor of the presented matrix is the identity:
2581	  \(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)_{\bullet,\, I} = 1_{d\times d}\).
2582	\end{lemma}
2583	\begin{proof}
2584	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one,
2585	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2586	  By injectivity (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}) compare after
2587	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\). The minor presentation identity
2588	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}) at \(J = I\) presents the
2589	  \(I\)-chart composite against its own inverse, which is the identity morphism, i.e.\
2590	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(1_{d\times d})\) (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}).
2591	\end{proof}

2593	\begin{lemma}[The classifying map realises the universal matrix as the presented matrix]
2594	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix}
2595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix}
2596	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_presentedMatrix,
2597	    lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2598	  Applying entrywise the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra map \(\mathrm{aeval}(M)\) -- which sends
2599	  the free variables of \(X^I\) to the complementary entries of
2600	  \(M = \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)\) -- to the universal matrix \(X^I\)
2601	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) returns \(M\):
2602	  \(\mathrm{aeval}(M)(X^I) = M\).
2603	\end{lemma}
2604	\begin{proof}
2605	  \uses{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2606	  Entrywise. On a complementary (free) entry \(x^I_{p,q}\) with \(q \notin I\),
2607	  \(\mathrm{aeval}(M)\) returns the corresponding entry of \(M\) by the variable-evaluation
2608	  rule. On an \(I\)-column entry the universal matrix has the identity minor, so the value
2609	  there is the corresponding entry of \(1_{d\times d}\), which matches the \(I\)-minor of
2610	  \(M\) -- the identity by \cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}.
2611	\end{proof}
2612	
2613	\begin{lemma}[Transport of the image matrix along a ring homomorphism]
2614	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom}
2615	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_ringHom}
2616	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix, lem:gr_minorDet_unit,
2617	    lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
2618	  For size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J\) and any ring homomorphism
2619	  \(\mathrm{incl} : R^I_J \to D\) out of the localised overlap ring whose composite with
2620	  the structure map \(R^I \to R^I_J\) equals \(g\), pushing the image matrix
2621	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forward along \(\mathrm{incl}\) yields \(\bigl((g_{*}X^I)_J\bigr)^{-1}
2622	  (g_{*}X^I)\), where \(g_{*}X^I\) is \(X^I\) base-changed along \(g\):
2623	  \[
2624	    \mathrm{incl}_{*}\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)
2625	      \;=\; \bigl((g_{*}X^I)_J\bigr)^{-1}\, (g_{*}X^I).
2626	  \]
2627	\end{lemma}
2628	\begin{proof}
2629	  \uses{lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
2630	  The image matrix is the product of the Cramer inverse \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\)
2631	  (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with \(X^I\); ring homomorphisms commute with matrix
2632	  multiplication, with the submatrix (minor) operation, and -- because the minor
2633	  determinant \(P^I_J\) is a unit (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}) -- with the nonsingular
2634	  inverse (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}). Pushing each factor forward along
2635	  \(\mathrm{incl}\) and rewriting the structure-map composite as \(g\) gives the stated
2636	  base-changed expression.
2637	\end{proof}

2639	\begin{lemma}[Naturality of the chart morphism]
2640	  \label{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism}
2641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.comp_chartMorphism}
2642	  \uses{def:gr_chartMorphism, lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix,
2643	    lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}
2644	  For \(p : W \to T_I\) on which the pulled-back \(I\)-chart composite is invertible, the
2645	  composite \(p \circ \varphi_I : W \to U^I\) is the morphism classified by the matrix
2646	  presenting the quotient along \(p \circ \iota_{T_I}\) -- i.e.\ classified by
2647	  \(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, p \circ \iota_{T_I}, I)\).
2648	\end{lemma}
2649	\begin{proof}
2650	  \uses{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}
2651	  Naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\Spec\) classifying map turns \(p \circ \varphi_I\) into
2652	  the morphism classified by the \(p\)-restriction of the chart matrix. The chart matrix is
2653	  the presented matrix along \(\iota_{T_I}\) (\cref{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}),
2654	  and its \(p\)-restriction is the presented matrix along \(p \circ \iota_{T_I}\)
2655	  (\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}); the two classifying maps therefore coincide.
2656	\end{proof}
2657	
2658	\begin{lemma}[Chart-point identity over a commutative ring]
2659	  \label{lem:gr_chart_point_eq}
2660	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chart_point_eq}
2661	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
2662	  Let \(K\) be a commutative ring and \(f : R^I \to K\) a ring map under which the minor
2663	  \(P^I_J\) is a unit. Then the transported classifying map
2664	  \(\mathrm{lift}(f) \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\) presents the same \(K\)-point through chart
2665	  \(J\) as \(f\) does through chart \(I\):
2666	  \[
2667	    \Spec(\mathrm{lift}(f) \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}) \circ \iota_J
2668	      \;=\; \Spec(f) \circ \iota_I.
2669	  \]
2670	\end{lemma}
2671	\begin{proof}
2672	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
2673	  Factor \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as the chart transition followed by the chart inclusion
2674	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}); the glue condition of the glue data
2675	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) makes the transition square commute, and the localisation
2676	  lift recovers \(f\) after the chart inclusion. Assembling the two reductions identifies
2677	  the two composites into the glued scheme.
2678	\end{proof}
2679	
2680	\begin{lemma}[Overlap compatibility of the chart morphisms]
2681	  \label{lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}
2682	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMorphism_glue_compat}
2683	  \uses{def:gr_chartMorphism, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp,
2684	    lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism, lem:gr_presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis,
2685	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom,
2686	    lem:gr_chart_point_eq, lem:gr_isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect,
2687	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}
2688	  On the intersection \(P = T_I \times_T T_J\) the two composites of the chart morphisms
2689	  with the glue-data immersions agree:
2690	  \[
2691	    \mathrm{pr}_1 \circ (\varphi_I \circ \iota_I)
2692	      \;=\; \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ (\varphi_J \circ \iota_J).
2693	  \]
2694	  This is the gluing hypothesis from which \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(x)\) is
2695	  assembled.
2696	\end{lemma}
2697	\begin{proof}
2698	  \uses{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp, lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism,
2699	    lem:gr_presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix,
2700	    lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom, lem:gr_chart_point_eq,
2701	    lem:gr_isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}
2702	  On \(P\) both chart composites pull back to isomorphisms
2703	  (\cref{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}), so the pulled-back quotient is presented by both
2704	  matrices \(M^I_P\) and \(M^J_P\); through \cref{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism} the two
2705	  composites are classified by these matrices. The \(J\)-minor of \(M^I_P\) is invertible
2706	  (\cref{lem:gr_isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect},
2707	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}), and the change-of-basis identity
2708	  \(M^I_P = (M^I_P)_J \cdot M^J_P\) (\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis}) --
2709	  realised on the universal matrices via
2710	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix} and the image-matrix transport
2711	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom} -- identifies the \(J\)-classifying ring map with
2712	  the localisation-transport \(\mathrm{lift}(\mathrm{aeval}\,M^I_P) \circ
2713	  \tilde\theta_{I,J}\) of the \(I\)-classifying map. The glue-condition identity
2714	  (\cref{lem:gr_chart_point_eq}) then closes the square into the glued scheme.
2715	\end{proof}
